summary.brief: Reilly v. Rastegar. Medical malpractice case involving surgery orthopaedic.

evidence_index.text: In January 2022, Reilly underwent spinal surgery while in the care of Dr. Rastegar, an orthopedic spine surgeon at Atrium.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: During Reilly’s surgical case, however, McIntyre handed Dr. Rastegar an inserter without a depth stop, and Dr. Rastegar inserted the implant too deeply, which resulted in Reilly’s spinal cord injury.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Moshifar opined that Dr. Rastegar had deviated from the orthopedic spine surgery standard of care resulting in Reilly’s injury.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: Because genuine issues of material fact remained as to whether McIntyre was negligent in failing to follow Dr. Rastegar’s orders regarding his tray instruments and whether that negligence caused Reilly’s injury, the trial court erred in granting summary judgment in favor of Atrium on Reilly’s claim for respondeat superior liability.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: We conclude that the reasoning in Czarney is applicable to this case. Although there is evidence that this order was not followed.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: Dr. Rastegar testified during his deposition that he had never used an inserter without a depth stop during ACDF cases and that he had communicated his instrument preference to 'everyone,' including hospital staff, like McIntyre.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Reilly’s assignment of error is sustained.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient scheduled for anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) surgery.

focused_exam.system: Pre-operative examination not detailed in judgment

initial_state.initial_working_problem: Cervical spine pathology requiring surgical intervention

requestables.description: Consultation note detailing surgical plan and instrument preferences

reveal.result_summary: Dr. Rastegar prefers inserter with depth stop for ACDF
reveal.clinical_significance: Ensures correct depth of implant insertion
reveal.decision_impact: Critical for preventing spinal cord injury

timeline_phases.name: Pre-operative Assessment
timeline_phases.description: Patient assessed and prepared for ACDF surgery

timeline_phases.name: Surgical Procedure
timeline_phases.description: Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion performed

decision_points.clinical_context: During ACDF surgery, selection of surgical instruments
decision_points.prompt: What type of inserter should be used for implant placement?

options.clinical_reasoning: Prevents over-insertion of implant into spinal canal
Increases risk of over-insertion and spinal cord injury

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant used inserter without depth stop

expected_action_court.description: Inserter with depth stop should have been used
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established preference and safety

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Use of incorrect inserter led to spinal cord injury
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Inserter with depth stop should have been used as per standard practice
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient sustained spinal cord injury due to over-insertion of implant
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Potential for chronic pain and neurological deficits

key_findings: Genuine issues of material fact regarding negligence

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Correct inserter would have prevented injury

court_findings.finding: Genuine issues of material fact regarding negligence
